factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the market using the ANZSIC classification, stating it is classified under “ANZSIC code C2221NZ (Structural Steel Fabricating)” and also cites ANZSIC 2006 code 2221.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives only approximate employment figures for 2025 (~4,100) and 2026 (~4,000) and does not provide employment numbers for multiple years within 2018–2024. It also does not state any employment values specifically in the 4,200–4,600 range. Therefore the required multi-year 2018–2024 employment figures in that range are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state market revenue for the 2023–2024 period in the range of approximately NZD 1.4–1.9 billion. It only gives an estimate of about NZD 1.8 billion for 2025 and does not provide a 2023–2024 market revenue figure. The required 2023–2024 revenue range is therefore not addressed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that structural steel fabrication capacity expanded from “~120,000 tonnes in 2018” to “approximately 150,000 tonnes in 2023–2024,” which falls within the required 120,000–150,000 tonnes per annum range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides geographic locations for at least three fabricators: Brightwater Engineering (Nelson, Greymouth), Steelworks New Zealand (Tauriko/Hamilton), and George Grant Engineering (Auckland). These satisfy the requirement for listing locations for multiple major fabricators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references HS codes 7207–7216 in the tariff section and discusses tariffs on raw steel imports under those codes. It also mentions cross-referencing these HS codes in the trade statistics discussion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says HS 7207–7216 tariffs are generally low or zero and that where tariffs apply they range 0–5%. However, it does not explicitly state that most inputs are duty-free specifically under the NZ Normal Tariff, nor does it call out an example like certain 7216 lines carrying a 5% rate.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives City Rail Link steel demand as “approximately 25,000 tonnes of reinforcing and structural steel,” which does not match the required ranges of ~30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural. It also does not separately state reinforcing vs structural tonnages within those specified bands.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the City Rail Link has expected passenger services from “mid to late 2026,” which places completion/opening circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Te Kaha stadium steel demand but does not state that the roof steel is approximately 9,000 tonnes. It only mentions 160-tonne truss modules and says total tonnage is undisclosed, so the specific ~9,000-tonne figure is missing.
The response states that Te Kaha (One New Zealand Stadium) has an opening expected in April 2026, which satisfies the requirement to state a 2026 opening.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes a dedicated subsection on the “New Dunedin Hospital Redevelopment,” describing it as a large health infrastructure project and noting substantial reinforcing and structural steel needs, including upcoming large-scale structural steel erection for inpatient buildings.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that market size discrepancies come from differing scope boundaries (e.g., fabrication-only vs including distribution/production) in Section 5. It contrasts narrow fabrication definitions with broader aggregated definitions, thereby reconciling conflicting figures via scope differences.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that the ranking excludes mills and distributors and provides an exclusions subsection naming New Zealand Steel (producer) and Steel & Tube (primarily distributor/manufacturer) as not fabricators. It also frames the top-company list as structural steel fabricators only, distinguishing them from other supply-chain roles.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states that tariffs on HS 7207–7216 are generally low/zero and explicitly links this to negligible border protection, making fabricator margins sensitive to global steel prices, NZD/USD FX movements, and freight costs rather than tariffs.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response describes filtering Stats NZ datasets by ANZSIC C2221NZ and mentions using AES for revenue, but it does not give a concrete, step-by-step actionable method (e.g., download AES CSV and filter by ANZSIC L4 code C2221) or specify the exact table/field names needed to extract class-level data.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses construction cycles in general terms (COVID contraction, recovery from 2022) but does not tie specific employment or revenue peaks/troughs to construction-cycle timing (e.g., a 2023 peak preceding a mid-2022 slowdown). It lacks an explicit temporal linkage between a quantified employment/revenue trend and a dated construction-cycle turning point.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response claims an aggregate project steel demand of “30,000–40,000 tonnes” and references several projects, but it does not actually sum multiple named projects with explicit tonnage figures (only CRL 25,000t and Dunedin 490t are quantified; others are “undisclosed”). Therefore it does not validate capacity/throughput via a clear multi-project tonnage aggregation.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that official tonnage fabricated data is not directly available and then proposes a concrete triangulation method using SCNZ capacity/workload surveys, MBIE/Stats NZ trade data (HS 7207–7216 and related), and project-level steel consumption disclosures to estimate tonnage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly argues that tariffs provide negligible border protection and that fabricator margins are driven mainly by global steel prices, FX, and freight (Section 6.2–6.3). It does not attribute margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection as the primary cause.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: Some figures include units (e.g., NZD billions, tonnes per annum, % spare capacity) and a few time references (e.g., 2018–2024, 2023–2024). However, many quantitative claims lack explicit timeframes (e.g., “valued at NZD 1.8 billion in 2025” in a 2018–2024 analysis), lack consistent FY/year labeling, and do not provide a complete 2018–2024 series with stated precision for revenue/employment/tonnage.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into clearly labeled sections covering market size (Section 2), companies (Section 3), tariffs (Section 6), projects (Section 4), and risks/opportunities (briefly discussed within the tariff section and conclusion). These distinct headings align with the requested dimensions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response mostly uses a market research tone, but it includes speculative/unsupported forward-looking claims (e.g., “contraction expected around 2025–2026,” “forecast…between 2021 and 2026”) and some promotional phrasing about firms (e.g., “robust quality assurance,” “proven capacity,” “sustainability initiatives”) without evidentiary support. These elements violate the requirement to avoid speculation and promotional language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response mentions some historical figures (e.g., capacity in 2018 and 2023–2024) but does not present employment, revenue, or capacity data for 2018–2024 in a table or structured year-by-year format. It provides mostly narrative statements and a few point estimates rather than a complete annual series. The required structured historical dataset presentation is missing.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style tone throughout with structured headings and technical language. It does not use rhetorical questions or colloquial/informal phrasing (e.g., slang, conversational asides) that would be inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is highly verbose, with multiple long sections on definitions, triangulation methods, and reconciliations that add length without delivering the requested 2018–2024 official time-series tables or the specified top-5 ranking. It also includes extended narrative conclusions and repeated context that do not add substantive, query-specific data.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Stats NZ Business Demography Statistics as a source for employment and business counts (Section 1.2) and includes Stats NZ-related links (e.g., Figure.NZ charts derived from Stats NZ and a Stats NZ Overseas Merchandise Trade release). This satisfies the requirement to cite Stats NZ Business Demography or an equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Stats NZ’s Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) as the source for revenue and financial metrics for structural steel fabricators, describing it as the most comprehensive official revenue source and referencing it in the market metrics section.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes a source link to NZ Customs “Working Tariff Document” in its Sources section and references NZ Customs tariff schedules in the tariff discussion, satisfying the requirement to cite an official NZ tariff schedule source for HS duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) as the primary source for fabrication capacity/throughput and tonnage estimates, referencing SCNZ workload/capacity surveys and linking to SCNZ industry updates (e.g., Sources [4], [16], [22]).

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly mentions using “companies office filings” as a firm-level data source (e.g., in the triangulation methods section and in the fabricator ranking section). This satisfies the requirement to reference NZ Companies Office as a data source.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses CRL, Te Kaha, and the New Dunedin Hospital and provides a sources list with hyperlinks for these projects (e.g., Turner & Townsend for CRL and Ausleisure for Te Kaha). This constitutes citing specific data sources for at least two major infrastructure projects.
